title: A thorium-229 optical nuclear clock with feedback loop
authors: L. Toscani De Col, T. Riebner, I. Morawetz, F. Schneider, N. Sempelmann, J. Schlachet-Lépinay, F. Schaden, M. Bartokos, G. A. Kazakov, K. Beeks, B. Gerstenecker, M. Pimon, S. Lahs, A. Hellerschmied, T. Lercher, J. Premper, A. Niessner, M. Matus, H. Denker, M. Cizek, O. Cip, V. Lal, G. Zitzer, V. Petrov, J. Tiedau, M. V. Okhapkin, E. Peik, T. Schumm
abstract: The laser-accessible nuclear transition in the thorium-229 isotope has been identified as a promising candidate for the realization of an optical nuclear clock. Such a nuclear clock might rival or outperform current optical clocks based on electron-shell transitions in atoms or ions, is expected to be more robust against external perturbations, and provides enhanced sensitivity in clock-based tests of fundamental principles of physics. Here, we implement a thorium-229 nuclear clock by stabilizing a continuous-wave laser to the 148 nm nuclear transition with rapid feedback based on continuous absorption spectroscopy. The thorium-229 nuclei are embedded into a millimeter-sized, room temperature calcium fluoride crystal. A subharmonic of the 148 nm radiation is continuously compared to a Yb+ single-ion clock. The nuclear clock shows a simple shot-noise limited scaling of the fractional frequency instability of $3\cdot 10^{-12} \sqrt{τ/\text{s}}$ where $τ$ is the averaging time, approaching $10^{-15}$ instabilities over 1 day of continuous operation. Improvements of the instability by several orders of magnitude can be projected for future solid-state nuclear clocks. We use the nuclear clock to constrain models of ultralight dark matter by searching for periodic fluctuations and slow drifts in the nuclear transition energy, on time scales between 20 s and 1 day. Drawing benefit from the enhanced sensitivity of the thorium-229 transition, these constraints compete with the best atomic clocks concerning dark matter coupling to photons and go beyond previous measurements regarding coupling to the strong force and quarks.
summary: This paper details the implementation and characterization of a highly sensitive optical nuclear clock based on the $	ext{Th-229}$ isotope. By stabilizing the laser to the nuclear transition and comparing it to an atomic clock, the researchers achieved state-of-the-art stability. This precision allows them to place world-leading constraints on variations in fundamental constants and search for signatures of ultralight dark matter.
main_problem: To realize and characterize an optical nuclear clock using the Thorium-229 isotope to test fundamental physics principles and search for dark matter signals.
main_result: The clock achieved a shot-noise limited instability approaching $10^{-15}$ over one day, setting stringent constraints on variations in fundamental constants and dark matter coupling.
method: The experiment involves stabilizing a continuous-wave laser to the $	ext{Th-229}$ nuclear transition frequency using rapid feedback based on continuous absorption spectroscopy, comparing it to a reference atomic clock ($	ext{Yb}^+$).
model_or_system: The system utilizes $	ext{Th-229}$ nuclei embedded in a $	ext{CaF}_2$ crystal, interrogating the $148 	ext{ nm}$ nuclear transition. The measurement technique involves sophisticated laser stabilization and frequency comparison against established atomic standards.
key_observables: Fractional frequency instability ($\sigma_y$), frequency ratio ($	ext{f}_{	ext{Th}}/	ext{f}_{	ext{Yb}^+}$), and the amplitude of periodic fluctuations in the beat frequency spectrum.
important_parameters: Instability approaching $10^{-15}/\sqrt{	au}/	ext{s}$; search time scales between $20 	ext{ s}$ and $1 	ext{ day}$; constraints on fundamental constants like $\alpha$ and $\Lambda_{QCD}$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the signal can be modeled by a Lorentzian fit for spectral analysis and relies on Monte Carlo simulations to establish detection thresholds for periodic signals.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup, including the laser stabilization loop and frequency comparison to the $	ext{Yb}^+$ clock. Other figures show the measured frequency instability ($\sigma_y$ vs $	au$) and exclusion plots for dark matter parameters.
paper_structure: The paper details the experimental setup and clock stabilization methods, followed by characterization of the clock's stability, and finally applying the high precision to search for variations in fundamental constants and dark matter signals using spectral analysis techniques.
why_interesting: This work represents a cutting-edge application of quantum metrology, pushing the limits of precision measurement by moving from electron-based atomic transitions to nuclear transitions, which offers unique sensitivity to fundamental physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic representation of the setup. a, Thorium-229 nuclear clock located at TU Wien. It is comprised of an ECDL, which serves as a clock laser, stabilized to a high-finesse cavity via the sideband of an EOM, and a seed laser which is frequency-quadrupled, followed by the last SBO-based VUV frequency generation doubling. The seed laser is modulated via the offset lock to generate the error signal from the absorption of the thorium-229 nuclei in the Th:CaF2 crystal, by readout of the PMT. The error signal is used as feedback to the EOM to compensate for the long-term drift of the cavity, closing the nuclear clock feedback loop. b, Frequency comparison to the Yb+ single-ion clock...

FIG. 2. Fractional frequency instability. The fractional fre- quency instability for different averaging times τ is shown for two clock operation modes. One mode stabilizes to the 5/2 →3/2 transition with T = 20 s between adjustment steps (red curve), the other with T = 30 min (blue curve). For τ > T, both curves follow the shot noise trend of the 5/2 →3/2 interrogation.

FIG. 3. Crystal position dependent center frequency. a, Deviations δfi of the Th-229 line centers from the frequency average of five consecutive measurements at different crystal positions. The second and fourth datasets (blue) were obtained by operating at the same crystal position. Each point is measured in T = 20 s. After ttot = 20 min of data taking, they average down to the respective dotted black lines. b, The table shows the line center deviation δfi for each scanning position. The indicated statistical uncertainties are determined through a Monte Carlo simulation of the clock loop (see Methods). The indicated linewidth at full width half maximum (FWHM) and absorption amplitudes A...

FIG. 4. Dark Matter exclusion plots. a, Lomb-Scargle periodogram calculated from the T = 20 s, ttot ≈23 h measurement of the beat frequency of the Th-229 nuclear clock and the Yb+ ion clock. We indicate the detection threshold that was not met at any frequency, as well as the exclusion band. We can exclude with 95% certainty that no oscillations with amplitudes above this value are present. b, Exclusion plot for scalar bosons with a mass mϕ and a coupling to photons with a dimensionless coupling constant de. The shaded areas have been excluded by various clock comparisons [45–50] under the assumption that the scalar boson constitutes the majority of the observed dark matter. Astrophysical...

FIG. 5. Calibration and principle of clock operation. a, Absorption measurement of the 5/2 →3/2 nuclear clock transition with an integration time T = 3 s per data point and an off-resonance frequency-offset of +300 kHz, expressed as a function of the offset lock frequency translated into the VUV. This is used to determine the linewidth for the error signal scan. b, Error signal measured with an integration time T = 3 s per data point and a constant frequency modulation of ∆fol = 34 kHz, from which the slope is extracted. c, Illustration of a clock frequency adjustment step determined by the fitted error signal shape.
